description: This figure presents thermodynamic equilibrium predictions for two steels labeled M1 and M2, showing how intercritical annealing (IA) temperature affects the equilibrium austenite fraction and austenite composition. Panel (a) plots the predicted equilibrium austenite fraction (γeq) versus temperature from ~625 °C to 900 °C, with the black dashed M1 curve lying above the red dashed M2 curve throughout the range. At the selected intercritical annealing temperature (IAT) of 700 °C, marked by a vertical dashed line, M1 reaches roughly 48 % austenite and M2 approximately 31 %. Panel (b) shows the corresponding predicted weight percentages of Mn, Al, Si, and C within austenite as functions of temperature. For both steels, Mn content decreases with increasing temperature to roughly 11 % at 700 °C, whereas Al increases moderately; C remains low and relatively stable, and Si appears only in M2. The caption and text together establish that this figure was used to select 700 °C as the IAT, balancing the desire for a high equilibrium austenite fraction against the need to retain sufficient Mn and C in austenite to prevent its decomposition to martensite during cooling. Consequently, 700 °C represents the thermodynamic compromise adopted for subsequent processing.
